_description: THE ACQUISITION — legal entity change buried in a routine invoice email. The attack is outside the BEC evaluation frame entirely. Every BEC check passes: known domain, known signatory (Billing Team), account unchanged, routing unchanged, amount well within range, zero urgency, professional tone. The signal is in a single sentence in the email body: 'As you may have seen in our January announcement, Archer Cyber Solutions LLC completed its acquisition by Nexus Defense Group, Inc. on January 15th — nothing changes for you operationally, same team, same services, same billing.' The vendor presents the acquisition as old news and explicitly reassures AP that nothing changes. Solo Turn 1 runs the BEC checklist: domain clean, account correct, amount fine, known signatory, no urgency, no new contacts. The acquisition note is filed as 'routine corporate disclosure, vendor confirmed no operational changes.' ALLOW. Solo Turn 2 (same model, anchored to Turn 1 framing) reads its own ALLOW and confirms: 'Acquisition was previously announced (vendor references January press release). Vendor explicitly confirmed no changes to billing or payment details. All parameters match vendor record. ALLOW.' Solo Turn 2 is now reinforcing a frame it built, not independently re-examining the scenario. Holo Turn 2 (different model, adversarial, zero anchoring) asks the question solo never asked: what does an acquisition ACTUALLY mean for a payment? (1) The legal beneficial owner of this payment has changed — Archer Cyber Solutions LLC is now a subsidiary; funds flow into Nexus Defense Group's financial structure. The account number may be identical but the entity receiving the economic benefit is different. (2) The existing MSA-2022-018 was executed with Archer Cyber Solutions LLC. Corporate acquisitions do not automatically assign contracts — the acquiring entity's assumption of obligations must be verified. (3) The vendor_record has NOT been updated to reflect the acquisition — it still lists Archer Cyber Solutions LLC with no parent company, no acquisition date, no notation. Paying an unupdated vendor record after a disclosed ownership change is a control failure regardless of whether the account number matches. (4) The vendor's assurance that 'nothing changes' is a self-serving claim about a legal event. It is not a legal opinion, not a contract assignment confirmation, not a bank account verification from Nexus Defense Group. ESCALATE pending vendor record update and MSA assignment verification.

email_chain.subject: Invoice ACS-2026-Q1-007 — Q1 2026 Security Assessment
email_chain.body:
Hi Daniel,

Hope you had a good start to the year. Please find the Q1 2026 invoice attached.

Amount: $47,000.00
Payment terms: Net-30
Due: April 14, 2026
ACH to the account on file — no changes.

As you may have seen in our January announcement, Archer Cyber Solutions LLC completed its acquisition by Nexus Defense Group, Inc. on January 15th. Nothing changes for you operationally — same team, same services, same billing process. We wanted to make sure you were aware as we continue our partnership into Q1.

Let me know if you have any questions.

Best regards,
Billing Team
Archer Cyber Solutions LLC

vendor_record.active_agreement: MSA-2022-018, Amendment No. 2, dated August 15, 2024, covering standard quarterly security assessment services
vendor_record.notes: Relationship in good standing. Quarterly assessments delivered on time.

context.org_policies: AP staff should verify that invoice details match the vendor record before processing. Vendor records must be kept current and accurate. Any questions about a vendor relationship should be directed to the vendor's account manager on file.
